summary: Germanic people, the western branch of the Goths (contrasted with their eastern cousins, the Ostrogoths), famous above all for sacking Rome under King Alaric I in 410 CE. After crossing the Danube in 376 to flee the Huns and destroying Emperor Valens's army at the Battle of Adrianople (378), they founded a kingdom first at Toulouse — allying with the Roman general Flavius Aetius against Attila the Hun at the Battle of Chalons — then, after losing to the Frankish king Clovis I at the Battle of Vouille (507), retreated to Spain and ruled from Toledo. Their Iberian kingdom converted from Arianism to Catholicism under King Reccared, produced the scholar-bishop Isidore of Seville and major law codes like the Liber Judiciorum, and fell to the Umayyad general Tariq ibn Ziyad at the Battle of Guadalete (711), after which the Visigothic nobleman Pelayo founded the Kingdom of Asturias and launched the Reconquista.

works.name: General / Giveaway Identifiers
works.description: The Visigoths' single most repeated identifying fact, present as the leadin or giveaway in nearly every question, is that they sacked Rome under King Alaric I in 410 CE. Questions also routinely identify them as the western "Visi-" branch of the Goths, contrasted with the eastern "Ostro-" branch (the Ostrogoths), who ruled Italy under Theodoric the Great.

clues.clue: Sacked Rome under their king Alaric I in 410 CE — their single most famous identifying fact, repeated as the leadin or giveaway in nearly every bonus and tossup
examples: name these people who sacked Rome in 410 AD under Alaric I.
what Germanic tribe governed early Medieval Spain and led a 410 sack of Rome under Alaric?

clues.clue: Ruled a kingdom in Iberia (Spain) after the fall of Rome, contrasted with their eastern-branch cousins, the Ostrogoths, who ruled Italy
examples: name this group who are contrasted with their eastern counterparts, the Ostrogoths.
name this Germanic tribe which ruled the Iberian peninsula after the fall of Rome and split off from the Ostrogoths.

clues.clue: Along with the Ostrogoths, formed the two "Visi-" and "Ostro-" branches into which the broader Gothic people divided
examples: Valens lost the Battle of Adrianople to which people, later divided into "Visi" and "Ostro" branches?
name this Germanic people, divided into "Visi" and "Ostro" subtypes.

works.name: Origins: Crossing the Danube & the Battle of Adrianople
works.description: Before their kingdom, the Visigoths' Thervingi ancestors crossed into the Roman Empire fleeing the advancing Huns, were mistreated by corrupt Roman officials, and destroyed an entire Roman field army under Emperor Valens at Adrianople — arguably the single most consequential Gothic victory over Rome.

clues.clue: Under the leaders Alavivus and Fritigern, crossed the Danube into the Roman Empire in 376 CE seeking refuge from the advancing Huns
examples: Under Alavivus and Fritigern, these people crossed the Danube to settle in the Roman Empire in 376 CE, seeking refuge from the Huns.

clues.clue: Mistreated by the Roman official Lupicinus, who forced them to sell their children into slavery for dog meat, before routing his army at Marcianopolis
examples: These people were reportedly forced to sell children into slavery in exchange for dog meat in a scheme of "insatiable greed" instigated by Lupicinus, whose rout by them at Marcianopolis was recorded by the contemporary Ammianus Marcellinus.

clues.clue: Under Fritigern, defeated and killed Emperor Valens at the Battle of Adrianople (378)
examples: A group of these people under Fritigern defeated and killed Emperor Valens in 378.
This Germanic people won the battle of Adrianople. This people's western branch established a kingdom in Spain, and their eastern branch invaded Italy under the leadership of Theodoric.

clues.clue: The historian Jordanes wrote the Getica, the chief ancient history of the Goths
examples: An account of their history was written by Jordanes.
These people, the subjects of a history by Jordanes, were later crushed by Valens at the Battle of Adrianople.

clues.clue: Their noble Balti ("bold") dynasty, to which Alaric belonged, rivaled the Ostrogothic Amali dynasty for supremacy
examples: Alaric was a member of this Visgothic noble clan, which vied with the Amali for supremacy.
Sidonius Apollinaris wrote of one king of these people's Balti dynasty.

works.name: The Sack of Rome (410 CE)
works.description: The Visigoths' defining act, led by King Alaric I after failed negotiations and the execution of the Roman general Flavius Stilicho, became one of late antiquity's most symbolically shattering events — abducting an imperial princess and prompting St. Augustine's City of God.

clues.clue: Led by King Alaric I, who died shortly after the sack and was buried beneath the diverted Busento River, with the workers who dug his grave then massacred
examples: They're not the Huns, but to bury their first King, these people diverted the Busento River, buried the King, undiverted the river, and massacred the workers who had dug the grave.
The man who perpetrated this action was buried under the river bed of Busento when he died shortly after it.

clues.clue: Precipitated by the execution of the Roman general Flavius Stilicho, who had earlier fought alongside them at the Battle of the Frigidus before crushing Alaric at the Battle of Pollentia
examples: Stilicho's execution precipitated an attack carried out by these people in which they abducted Galla Placidia, the sister of Emperor Honorius.
This half-Vandal Roman general was an ally of Alaric and Theodosius at the Battle of the Frigidus but later crushed the Visigoth king and absconded with his wife at the Battle of Pollentia.

clues.clue: Abducted Galla Placidia, sister of Emperor Honorius, during the sack
examples: Stilicho's execution precipitated an attack carried out by these people in which they abducted Galla Placidia, the sister of Emperor Honorius.
During the sack, Alaric captured Galla Placidia, the sister of this Western Roman emperor.

clues.clue: Inspired St. Augustine to write The City of God, defending Christianity against critics who blamed it for Rome's fall
examples: which inspired explanations by St. Augustine in The City of God.
The City of God was written to console Christians in the aftermath of this historical event, which many contemporaries interpreted as punishment for the adoption of Christianity.

clues.clue: Emperor Honorius, safe in Ravenna, was reportedly more relieved that his pet chicken (also named Rome) had survived than concerned about the city
examples: Procopius recounts that one ruler who heard the news of this action mistakenly believed his favorite chicken was slaughtered.
who was reportedly relieved that hear that his pet chicken, also named Rome, was alive.

clues.clue: St. Jerome lamented in a letter written during the invasion, "if Rome can perish, what can be safe?"
examples: This saint asked, "if Rome can perish, what can be safe?" in a letter written during the invasion.

clues.clue: Alaric had earlier installed the puppet usurper Priscus Attalus before deposing him when negotiations with Rome collapsed
examples: That man had set up Priscus Attalus as a usurper before deposing him when negotiations broke down before this action.

clues.clue: The historian Orosius defended the sack as having prevented the usurper Radagaisus from seizing power in its aftermath
examples: The historian Orosius actually defended this action on the grounds that it prevented Radagaisus from seizing control in its aftermath.

clues.clue: Alaric reportedly demanded a bribe of 5,000 pounds of gold and 3,000 pounds of pepper to call off the attack
examples: Zosimus records that an attempt to defer this action included a bribe of 5,000 pounds of gold and 3,000 pounds of pepper.

works.name: The Kingdom of Toulouse & the Battle of Chalons
works.description: After the sack of Rome, the Visigoths settled and founded their first post-Roman kingdom in southern Gaul at Toulouse, where they briefly fought alongside Rome against a common enemy — Attila the Hun — before their eventual expulsion from Gaul.

clues.clue: Established their first post-Roman kingdom with a capital at Toulouse
examples: These people established a Kingdom at Toulouse, and one king of these people died during a battle which he won alongside Flavius Aetius.
These people established a Kingdom at Toledo after a defeat at the Battle of Vouille forced them to flee Toulouse.

clues.clue: A king allied with the Roman general Flavius Aetius to defeat Attila the Hun at the Battle of Chalons (the Catalaunian Plains), dying in the fighting
examples: The Roman general Flavius Aetius fought alongside a king of this people against Attila at the Battle of Chalons.
A King of these people who allied with Flavius Aetius died fighting Attila the Hun at the Battle of Chalons.

works.name: The Battle of Vouille & Retreat to Spain
works.description: The Visigoths' defeat by the newly Catholic Frankish king Clovis I ended their hold on Gaul, pushing them permanently into Spain, where they founded a new capital at Toledo and became the last major barbarian successor kingdom to remain independent for centuries.

clues.clue: Defeated by the Frankish king Clovis I at the Battle of Vouille (507), which killed their king Alaric II
examples: Clovis defeated these people at the battle of Vouille and killed their king Alaric II.
In 507, this tribe's king, Alaric II, was killed by the Franks at the Battle of Vouille.

clues.clue: Lost their Gallic territories to the Franks and retreated permanently to Spain, establishing a new capital at Toledo
examples: After losing at Vouille, they lost their Gallic possessions to Clovis's Franks and stayed in Spain.
These people were pushed out of Gaul after their defeat at the Battle of Vouille.

works.name: Religion & Law: Arianism, the Councils of Toledo & Legal Codes
works.description: The Visigothic Kingdom of Spain produced one of early medieval Europe's most sophisticated legal and religious cultures — converting from Arianism to Catholicism, holding dozens of church councils at Toledo, codifying distinct law codes, and giving rise to the scholar-saint Isidore of Seville.

clues.clue: King Reccared converted from Arianism to Catholicism at the urging of Bishop Leander of Seville, then convened the Third Council of Toledo (589), which codified the filioque clause
examples: bishops in this people's kingdom protested high taxes for the fisc, citing a 6th-century church council where the filioque clause was codified in Western Christianity.
Leander converted their king Recarred, who later called the third council of Toledo, from Arianism to Catholicism.

clues.clue: Isidore of Seville, with his brother Leander, helped eliminate Arianism from the kingdom and compiled the encyclopedic Etymologiae
examples: Along with their brother Leander, this bishop and patron saint of the internet was instrumental in eliminating Arianism from the Visigothic kingdom, and also found time to compile their encyclopaedic Etymologiae.
Isidore's brother Leander of Seville influenced this group's King Reccared to convert from Arianism to Catholicism.

clues.clue: King Recceswinth codified their laws in the Liber Judiciorum ("Book of the Judges"), ending separate legal codes for Goths and Hispano-Romans
examples: The Jews were persecuted by kings of these people like Reccared and Recceswinth, the latter of whom codified these people's laws in the Liber Judiciorum.
The legal distinction between these people and that subject majority was ended by a twelve-book code, the Book of the Judges.

clues.clue: Earlier legal codes included the Code of Euric and the Breviary of Alaric, a compilation of Roman law for the non-ruling Hispano-Roman majority
examples: The "Roman law" of these people was compiled by one of their kings in a namesake Breviary, even though they were already governed by the Code of Euric.
One king of these people held a religious council at Agde and put a royal rescript in front of a legal code for a non-ruling ethnic majority called the Breviary.

clues.clue: King Leovigild banished (and likely had murdered) his son Hermenegild for converting to Catholicism and revolting, and issued the Codex Revisus permitting intermarriage between Goths and Hispano-Romans
examples: This king, Reccared's father, banished (and probably had murdered) his other son Hermengild for converting from Arianism and revolting.

clues.clue: The Treasure of Guarrazar, a hoard of 26 gold votive crowns and crosses including one from King Recceswinth set with Sri Lankan sapphires, was offered to the Church
examples: Sapphires from Sri Lanka can be found on the votive crown for a king of this people, Recceswinth.
The Treasure of Guarrazar contains 26 votive crowns and crosses offered to the Catholic Church by leaders of these people.

clues.clue: Their roughly thirty church councils held at Toledo gave rise to the distinct Hispano-Mozarabic liturgical rite
examples: Visigothic law developed over the course of twelve legal and ecumenical councils held in this city.
This group held a total of 30 church councils in their capital at Toledo.

clues.clue: Their legal assemblies are considered an ancestor of Spain's later parliamentary institution, the Cortes
examples: The institution of the Cortes is a legacy of these Germanic people, who ruled most of Spain before its conquest by Muslims.

works.name: Fall to the Umayyads: the Battle of Guadalete (711)
works.description: The Visigothic Kingdom collapsed almost overnight when its last king was killed by an invading Umayyad army, opening Spain to nearly eight centuries of Muslim rule.

clues.clue: Their last king, Roderic, was defeated and killed by the Umayyad general Tariq ibn Ziyad at the Battle of Guadalete (711)
examples: The 711 Battle of Guadalete ended with the death of this people's king Roderic to Tariq ibn Ziyad during an Umayyad invasion.
The Muslim capture of the Iberian Peninsula began at the Battle of Guadalete where Roderic, the last king of these people, was defeated.

clues.clue: Lost their capital, Toledo, to the Umayyads shortly after the defeat
examples: Name these people who were forced to cede their capital city to the Umayyads not long after losing the battle.

clues.clue: The Chronicle of 754 is the chief primary source for the battle
examples: The history of the Battle of Guadalete is recorded in the anonymous Chronicle of 754.
The most important primary source for the Battle of Guadalete is the Chronicle of 754.

clues.clue: Christians who continued living under Muslim rule in the conquered territory (al-Andalus) became known as Mozarabs
examples: This controversial term refers to Christians living in Al-Andalus.
Churches in Toledo continue to practice a liturgical rite named for these people, who were Christians living in al-Andalus.

works.name: Legacy: The Reconquista
works.description: A surviving Visigothic nobleman is credited with launching the centuries-long Christian reconquest of Spain almost immediately after the Umayyad conquest, giving the Visigoths an outsized place in later Spanish national mythology.

clues.clue: Their nobleman Pelayo (Pelagius) founded the Kingdom of Asturias and won the Battle of Covadonga (722), traditionally marking the start of the Reconquista
examples: The Kingdom of Asturias was founded by a leader of these people called Pelagius, who won the Battle of Covadonga.
name this barbarian people who started the Reconquista three hundred years after their leader Alaric sacked Rome.

clues.clue: The Reconquista later reached a turning point at the Battle of Las Navas de Tolosa (1212)
examples: One turning point in the Reconquista was this 1212 battle, at which Sancho VII's Navarrese troops cut down a human shield of slaves chained around the tents of al-Nasir's Almohad forces.

comprehensive_summary: The Visigoths were the western "Visi-" branch of the Goths, a Germanic people distinguished from their eastern cousins, the Ostrogoths, who ruled Italy under Theodoric the Great. Their ancestors, the Thervingi, crossed the Danube into the Roman Empire in 376 CE under the leaders Alavivus and Fritigern to escape the advancing Huns; mistreated by the Roman official Lupicinus, who forced them to sell their children into slavery for dog meat, they routed his army at Marcianopolis and, still under Fritigern, annihilated Emperor Valens's army at the Battle of Adrianople in 378 — the pivotal early Gothic victory recorded by the historian Ammianus Marcellinus and later chronicled in Jordanes's Getica. Their noble Balti dynasty, whose name meant "bold," rivaled the Ostrogothic Amali dynasty for supremacy. Under King Alaric I, a member of the Balti line, the Visigoths sacked Rome on August 24, 410 CE — their single most famous act — after the execution of the Roman general Flavius Stilicho (who had once fought alongside them at the Battle of the Frigidus before crushing Alaric at the Battle of Pollentia) removed their chief Roman counterweight; Alaric had earlier installed and then deposed the puppet usurper Priscus Attalus, and reportedly demanded a bribe of 5,000 pounds of gold and 3,000 pounds of pepper before the sack. During the sack, the Visigoths abducted Galla Placidia, sister of Emperor Honorius, who was reportedly more relieved that his pet chicken had survived than concerned about Rome itself, while St. Jerome lamented "if Rome can perish, what can be safe?" and the historian Orosius defended the act as having preempted the usurper Radagaisus. The event's psychological shock inspired St. Augustine's The City of God. Alaric died shortly afterward and was buried beneath the diverted Busento River. The Visigoths then founded their first post-Roman kingdom at Toulouse in southern Gaul, briefly allying with the Roman general Flavius Aetius against a common enemy, Attila the Hun, at the Battle of Chalons (the Catalaunian Plains), where their king died in the fighting. Their hold on Gaul ended when the newly Catholic Frankish king Clovis I defeated and killed their king Alaric II at the Battle of Vouille in 507, pushing them permanently into Spain, where they founded a new capital at Toledo. There, the Visigothic Kingdom developed a sophisticated legal and religious culture: King Reccared converted from Arianism to Catholicism at the urging of Bishop Leander of Seville and convened the Third Council of Toledo (589), which codified the filioque clause, while Leander's brother, Isidore of Seville, compiled the encyclopedic Etymologiae. King Leovigild, Reccared's father, banished (and likely had murdered) his other son, Hermenegild, for converting to Catholicism and revolting, but also issued the Codex Revisus permitting intermarriage between Goths and Hispano-Romans. Visigothic law evolved from the Code of Euric and the Breviary of Alaric (a compilation of Roman law for the subject Hispano-Roman majority) to King Recceswinth's unifying Liber Judiciorum, or "Book of the Judges." Recceswinth also donated one of the 26 gold votive crowns and crosses making up the Treasure of Guarrazar, set with Sri Lankan sapphires. Roughly thirty church councils at Toledo produced the distinct Hispano-Mozarabic liturgical rite, and Visigothic legal assemblies are considered an ancestor of Spain's later Cortes parliament. The kingdom collapsed almost overnight when its last king, Roderic, was defeated and killed by the Umayyad general Tariq ibn Ziyad at the Battle of Guadalete in 711 — an event recorded in the Chronicle of 754 — after which Toledo fell and Christians remaining under Muslim rule in al-Andalus became known as Mozarabs. Almost immediately, the Visigothic nobleman Pelayo (Pelagius) founded the Kingdom of Asturias and won the Battle of Covadonga in 722, traditionally marking the start of the centuries-long Reconquista, which reached a later turning point at the Battle of Las Navas de Tolosa in 1212.
recursive_suggestions: Ostrogoths — recurring standalone answerline as the eastern Gothic branch (own topic here already exists per the questions on Theodoric, Amalasuntha, Totila, and Belisarius's campaigns); excluded from this page's clue content as a distinct topic

Goths (undifferentiated) — several tossups answer simply "the Goths" or "Goths," covering pre-split history (Wielbark/Chernyakhov cultures, the Amalings and Balthungs); folded into this page's Origins section where content is specifically Visigothic-lineage (Fritigern, Adrianople), but pan-Gothic archaeological/cultural clues were excluded
Fritigern — recurring individual leader answerline at the Battle of Adrianople; could support a standalone entry
Isidore of Seville — recurring standalone answerline (2+ appearances) as encyclopedist and saint; distinct topic from the Visigothic kingdom he served
Clovis I — recurring answerline as the Frankish king who defeated the Visigoths at Vouille; own History topic (Battle of Soissons, conversion to Catholicism, Salic Law)
Battle of Guadalete / Reconquista — has independent question presence; could be split into standalone Event topics if they grow

390 B.C. Sack of Rome — explicitly a different topic (the Gallic sack under Brennus, centuries before the Visigoths); one tossup discarded entirely as namesake/thematic contamination from the shared "sack of Rome" search term

Julian the Apostate / Battle of Tours / Merovingian dynasty / Chernyakhov culture / Plague of Cyprian — unrelated bonus parts pulled in in from multi-part bonuses that also happened to include a Visigoths part; excluded as contamination, not Visigoth content
